content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:60036106

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains stable user/business profile details, dated prior marketing outcomes, and reusable marketing strategy context; force_memory_write requires a non-noop route.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements in the transcript refer to the stable user entity; no existing candidates provided.
- mention=Instagram; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Instagram; reason=Concrete reusable service mentioned repeatedly in relation to engagement and Stories.
- mention=Instagram Stories; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Instagram Stories; reason=Concrete reusable Instagram feature discussed as a content channel.
- mention=Facebook; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=SERVICE; name=Facebook; reason=Concrete reusable service mentioned in the context of ad campaigns.
- mention=Facebook ad campaign; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Facebook ad campaign; reason=Reusable marketing concept central to the user's described past and planned advertising work.
- mention=handmade jewelry; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=handmade jewelry; reason=Concrete product category that is the user's niche and product type.
- mention=unique accessories; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=unique accessories; reason=Reusable product/style category keyword related to the user's niche.
- mention=indie fashion; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=indie fashion; reason=Reusable fashion niche keyword related to the user's target audience.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|handmade jewelry business|Instagram|Facebook ad campaign|Instagram Stories
        text_queries=handmade jewelry target audience young adults unique accessories indie fashion Instagram engagement|influencer promoted product to 10000 followers great success|Instagram story production process feedback polls favorite product design 50 votes|previous Facebook ad campaign reached 2000 people 50 clicks website similar products
        predicate_hints=responsible_for|has_goal|current_metric_value|has_experience_with|works_with

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:1c1c34b5324a2c15b11d7e6e83dcefa7] EXTERNAL

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 10:32
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to improve my Instagram engagement. Can you suggest some popular hashtags in my niche that I can use? By the way, I recently collaborated with an influencer who promoted my product to her 10,000 followers, and it was a great success.
assistant: Congratulations on the successful collaboration! That's a great way to reach a new audience.

To help you with popular hashtags in your niche, I'll need to know a bit more about your product or the industry you're in. Could you please share some details about your product or niche? For example:

1. What type of product or service do you offer?
2. Who is your target audience?
3. What are the main keywords related to your product or service?

Once I have a better understanding of your niche, I can suggest some popular and relevant hashtags for you to use.

Also, kudos on collaborating with an influencer! That's a great way to tap into their audience and increase brand awareness. You might also consider engaging with their followers by responding to comments and direct messages to build relationships and encourage user-generated content.

Remember to keep your hashtags relevant, and don't overdo it. Using 5-10 niche-specific hashtags per post is a good starting point. You can also experiment with different hashtags to find the ones that work best for your content.

Looking forward to hearing more about your product or niche!
user: My product is a handmade jewelry, and my target audience is young adults who are interested in unique accessories. Some keywords related to my product are "handmade jewelry", "unique accessories", and "indie fashion".
assistant: Lovely! Handmade jewelry is a great niche with a lot of potential for creative expression and individuality.

Here are some popular hashtags that you can use to reach your target audience:

**Handmade Jewelry**

* #handmadejewelry (1.4M+ posts)
* #handmadeshop (434k+ posts)
* #handcraftedjewelry (231k+ posts)
* #jewelrymaking (185k+ posts)
* #handmadewithlove (143k+ posts)

**Unique Accessories**

* #uniqueaccessories (211k+ posts)
* #accessorize (174k+ posts)
* #jewelrylover (144k+ posts)
* #fashionaccessories (134k+ posts)
* # statementaccessories (114k+ posts)

**Indie Fashion**

* #indiefashion (231k+ posts)
* #alternativefashion (143k+ posts)
* #bohofashion (124k+ posts)
* #indiestyle (114k+ posts)
* #fashionrebel (93k+ posts)

**Additional Tips**

1. Use a mix of niche-specific hashtags (e.g., #handmadejewelry) and broader fashion-related hashtags (e.g., #fashionaccessories) to reach a wider audience.
2. Create a unique branded hashtag for your jewelry brand and encourage your followers to use it when sharing photos of your products. This will help you track user-generated content and build a community around your brand.
3. Post high-quality photos that showcase your jewelry in a lifestyle setting, such as on a person or with other fashion items. This will help potential customers visualize how your jewelry can be worn and styled.
4. Engage with your followers by responding to comments and direct messages. This will help build relationships and encourage loyalty to your brand.
5. Consider collaborating with other handmade jewelry makers or indie fashion influencers to reach new audiences and build your network.

Remember to keep your hashtags relevant to the content of your post, and don't overdo it. Using 5-10 hashtags per post is a good starting point. Good luck, and I hope these hashtags help you increase your Instagram engagement!
user: I'd like to know more about how I can create an Instagram story that will engage my followers. Can you give me some ideas on what type of content I can post on my story?
assistant: Instagram Stories are a fantastic way to engage with your followers, share behind-the-scenes content, and build a more personal connection with your audience. Here are some ideas for content you can post on your Instagram Story:

**Behind-the-Scenes (BTS) Content**

1. **Sneak peeks**: Give your followers a first look at new jewelry designs, collections, or upcoming promotions.
2. **Design process**: Share how you create your handmade jewelry, from conceptualization to finishing touches.
3. **Studio tours**: Take your followers on a tour of your workspace, showcasing your tools, materials, and creative process.

**Interactive Content**

1. **Polls**: Ask your followers to vote on their favorite jewelry design, color palette, or style.
2. **Q&A**: Host a Q&A session, answering questions about your jewelry, design process, or inspiration.
3. **Guess the price**: Share a photo of a new piece and ask followers to guess the price.

**Exclusive Offers and Promotions**

1. **Limited-time discounts**: Offer a special promo code or discount exclusively to your Instagram followers.
2. **Flash sales**: Create a sense of urgency with a limited-time sale or special offer.
3. **Sneak peek sales**: Give your followers early access to new products or collections.

**Lifestyle and Inspiration**

1. **Styling tips**: Share how to style your jewelry with different outfits or for different occasions.
2. **Customer showcases**: Feature your followers wearing your jewelry and share their stories.
3. **Mood boards**: Create a visual board with inspiring images, colors, and textures that reflect your brand's aesthetic.

**Personal and Authentic Content**

1. **Meet the maker**: Share a bit about yourself, your inspiration, and your passion for jewelry making.
2. **Bloopers and outtakes**: Show the humorous side of your design process or studio mishaps.
3. **Personal stories**: Share how your jewelry has impacted your life or the lives of your customers.

**Other Ideas**

1. **Collaborations**: Partner with other handmade jewelry makers or influencers to create exclusive content.
2. **Tutorials**: Share simple jewelry-making techniques or styling tips.
3. **Giveaways**: Host a giveaway or contest to encourage engagement and attract new followers.

**Tips and Best Practices**

1. **Use relevant hashtags**: Add relevant hashtags to your Instagram Story to increase visibility.
2. **Keep it concise**: Keep your Story content brief and engaging, with a maximum of 15 seconds per clip.
3. **Use stickers and GIFs**: Add fun and interactive elements to your Story using stickers, GIFs, and emojis.
4. **Post consistently**: Maintain a consistent posting schedule to keep your followers engaged and interested in your content.
5. **Monitor and respond**: Keep an eye on your Story's performance and respond to comments and messages promptly.

By incorporating these ideas into your Instagram Story strategy, you'll be able to engage your followers, build a loyal community, and showcase your unique handmade jewelry brand.
user: I've been using Instagram stories to give my followers a sneak peek into my production process, and to ask for their feedback on new products. I've also been experimenting with polls, like the one I did last week where I asked my followers to vote for their favorite product design. Around 50 people voted, which was a great response.
assistant: That's fantastic! 🎉 You're doing a great job of engaging your followers and making them feel involved in your design process. By sharing behind-the-scenes content and asking for feedback, you're building a sense of community and fostering loyalty among your audience.

The poll you conducted is a great example of this. With 50 votes, you've not only generated engagement but also gathered valuable feedback that can inform your design decisions. This type of interaction can help you understand your audience's preferences and create products that resonate with them.

Here are a few suggestions to take your Instagram Stories to the next level:

1. **Use the "Question" sticker**: In addition to polls, you can use the "Question" sticker to ask open-ended questions and gather more detailed feedback from your followers.
2. **Share exclusive content**: Consider offering exclusive deals, promo codes, or early access to new products to your Instagram followers to make them feel special and valued.
3. **Create a series**: Develop a series of Instagram Stories that showcase a specific aspect of your production process, such as a "Design of the Week" or "Material of the Month" feature.
4. **Collaborate with followers**: Encourage followers to share their own jewelry-making experiences or tips, and re-share them on your Story. This can help create a sense of community and social proof.
5. **Utilize Instagram's features**: Experiment with Instagram's features like IGTV, Reels, and Live Streaming to mix up your content and keep your followers engaged.

Remember to track your Instagram Insights to see which types of content are performing well and adjust your strategy accordingly. Keep up the great work, and I'm sure you'll continue to see engagement and growth on your Instagram account! 💡
user: I'm planning to run another Facebook ad campaign soon, and I'd like to target people who have shown interest in similar products to mine. Can you give me some tips on how to effectively target my ad audience on Facebook?
assistant: Facebook ads can be a powerful way to reach potential customers who are interested in products like yours. Here are some tips to help you effectively target your ad audience on Facebook:

**1. Define Your Target Audience**

Before creating your ad campaign, identify your ideal customer based on demographics, interests, behaviors, and connections. Consider factors like:

* Age, gender, location, and language
* Interests, hobbies, and activities related to your product
* Behaviors, such as online shopping habits or purchase history
* Connections, like friends of fans or people who have engaged with your content

**2. Use Facebook's Audience Insights Tool**

Facebook's Audience Insights tool provides valuable information about your target audience, including demographics, interests, and behaviors. Use this tool to gain a deeper understanding of your audience and create more targeted ads.

**3. Leverage Lookalike Audiences**

Lookalike Audiences allow you to target people who are similar to your existing customers or followers. This can be a powerful way to reach new audiences who are likely to be interested in your product.

**4. Target Based on Interests**

Target people who have shown interest in products or topics related to yours. For example, if you sell handmade jewelry, you could target people who have liked pages related to fashion, accessories, or jewelry.

**5. Use Custom Audiences**

Custom Audiences allow you to target people who have engaged with your content, visited your website, or made a purchase from you in the past. This can help you retarget people who have already shown interest in your product.

**6. Exclude Unwanted Audiences**

Use Facebook's exclusion targeting feature to exclude people who are unlikely to be interested in your product. For example, if you're targeting people interested in handmade jewelry, you might exclude people who have shown interest in mass-produced jewelry.

**7. Monitor and Optimize**

Monitor your ad performance regularly and optimize your targeting based on the results. Identify which audiences are performing well and adjust your targeting accordingly.

**8. Use Facebook's Automatic Placements**

Facebook's Automatic Placements feature allows you to target multiple platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, and Audience Network, with a single ad campaign. This can help you reach a wider audience and increase ad visibility.

**9. Consider Retargeting**

Retargeting ads can be an effective way to reach people who have visited your website or engaged with your content but haven't converted yet. Use Facebook's retargeting feature to target these people and encourage them to take action.

**10. Test and Refine**

Test different targeting options and refine your approach based on the results. Continuously monitor your ad performance and adjust your targeting to optimize your ad spend and improve your ROI.

By following these tips, you can create targeted Facebook ad campaigns that reach people who are interested in products like yours and drive conversions for your business. Good luck with your ad campaign!
user: I'm planning to target people who have shown interest in similar products to mine, just like I did in my previous Facebook ad campaign, which reached around 2,000 people and resulted in 50 clicks to my website.
assistant: It sounds like you have a good understanding of your target audience and have had some success with Facebook ads in the past. Targeting people who have shown interest in similar products to yours is a great way to reach potential customers who are already interested in products like yours.

Considering your previous campaign reached 2,000 people and resulted in 50 clicks to your website, that's a decent click-through rate (CTR) of 2.5%. To improve upon this, you may want to consider a few things:

**1. Refine your targeting**: Take a closer look at the interests and behaviors of the people who clicked on your ad and converted. Are there any common themes or patterns? Use this information to refine your targeting and reach an even more specific audience.

**2. Optimize your ad creative**: Review your ad creative, including the image, headline, and copy. Is it eye-catching and compelling? Are you using social proof, such as customer testimonials or reviews, to build credibility? Consider testing different ad creative to see what resonates best with your audience.

**3. Improve your landing page experience**: When people click on your ad, they should be taken to a landing page that is relevant to the ad and provides a seamless user experience. Make sure your landing page is optimized for conversions, with a clear call-to-action (CTA) and minimal distractions.

**4. Consider increasing your budget**: If you're happy with the performance of your previous campaign, you may want to consider increasing your budget to reach a larger audience and drive more conversions.

**5. Monitor and optimize regularly**: Keep a close eye on your ad performance and make adjustments as needed. This could include pausing underperforming ads, adjusting your targeting, or testing new ad creative.

By refining your targeting, optimizing your ad creative, improving your landing page experience, and monitoring your performance regularly, you can continue to drive conversions and grow your business.

What do you think is the most important factor in driving conversions for your business? Is it the quality of your product, the strength of your brand, or something else entirely?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:60036106

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains stable user/business profile details, dated prior marketing outcomes, and reusable marketing strategy context; force_memory_write requires a non-noop route.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to improve my Instagram engagement. Can you suggest some popular hashtags in my niche that I can use? By the way, I recently collaborated with an influencer who promoted my product to her 10,000 followers, and it was a great success.
assistant: Congratulations on the successful collaboration! That's a great way to reach a new audience.

To help you with popular hashtags in your niche, I'll need to know a bit more about your product or the industry you're in. Could you please share some details about your product or niche? For example:

1. What type of product or service do you offer?
2. Who is your target audience?
3. What are the main keywords related to your product or service?

Once I have a better understanding of your niche, I can suggest some popular and relevant hashtags for you to use.

Also, kudos on collaborating with an influencer! That's a great way to tap into their audience and increase brand awareness. You might also consider engaging with their followers by responding to comments and direct messages to build relationships and encourage user-generated content.

Remember to keep your hashtags relevant, and don't overdo it. Using 5-10 niche-specific hashtags per post is a good starting point. You can also experiment with different hashtags to find the ones that work best for your content.

Looking forward to hearing more about your product or niche!
user: My product is a handmade jewelry, and my target audience is young adults who are interested in unique accessories. Some keywords related to my product are "handmade jewelry", "unique accessories", and "indie fashion".
assistant: Lovely! Handmade jewelry is a great niche with a lot of potential for creative expression and individuality.

Here are some popular hashtags that you can use to reach your target audience:

**Handmade Jewelry**

* #handmadejewelry (1.4M+ posts)
* #handmadeshop (434k+ posts)
* #handcraftedjewelry (231k+ posts)
* #jewelrymaking (185k+ posts)
* #handmadewithlove (143k+ posts)

**Unique Accessories**

* #uniqueaccessories (211k+ posts)
* #accessorize (174k+ posts)
* #jewelrylover (144k+ posts)
* #fashionaccessories (134k+ posts)
* # statementaccessories (114k+ posts)

**Indie Fashion**

* #indiefashion (231k+ posts)
* #alternativefashion (143k+ posts)
* #bohofashion (124k+ posts)
* #indiestyle (114k+ posts)
* #fashionrebel (93k+ posts)

**Additional Tips**

1. Use a mix of niche-specific hashtags (e.g., #handmadejewelry) and broader fashion-related hashtags (e.g., #fashionaccessories) to reach a wider audience.
2. Create a unique branded hashtag for your jewelry brand and encourage your followers to use it when sharing photos of your products. This will help you track user-generated content and build a community around your brand.
3. Post high-quality photos that showcase your jewelry in a lifestyle setting, such as on a person or with other fashion items. This will help potential customers visualize how your jewelry can be worn and styled.
4. Engage with your followers by responding to comments and direct messages. This will help build relationships and encourage loyalty to your brand.
5. Consider collaborating with other handmade jewelry makers or indie fashion influencers to reach new audiences and build your network.

Remember to keep your hashtags relevant to the content of your post, and don't overdo it. Using 5-10 hashtags per post is a good starting point. Good luck, and I hope these hashtags help you increase your Instagram engagement!
user: I'd like to know more about how I can create an Instagram story that will engage my followers. Can you give me some ideas on what type of content I can post on my story?
assistant: Instagram Stories are a fantastic way to engage with your followers, share behind-the-scenes content, and build a more personal connection with your audience. Here are some ideas for content you can post on your Instagram Story:

**Behind-the-Scenes (BTS) Content**

1. **Sneak peeks**: Give your followers a first look at new jewelry designs, collections, or upcoming promotions.
2. **Design process**: Share how you create your handmade jewelry, from conceptualization to finishing touches.
3. **Studio tours**: Take your followers on a tour of your workspace, showcasing your tools, materials, and creative process.

**Interactive Content**

1. **Polls**: Ask your followers to vote on their favorite jewelry design, color palette, or style.
2. **Q&A**: Host a Q&A session, answering questions about your jewelry, design process, or inspiration.
3. **Guess the price**: Share a photo of a new piece and ask followers to guess the price.

**Exclusive Offers and Promotions**

1. **Limited-time discounts**: Offer a special promo code or discount exclusively to your Instagram followers.
2. **Flash sales**: Create a sense of urgency with a limited-time sale or special offer.
3. **Sneak peek sales**: Give your followers early access to new products or collections.

**Lifestyle and Inspiration**

1. **Styling tips**: Share how to style your jewelry with different outfits or for different occasions.
2. **Customer showcases**: Feature your followers wearing your jewelry and share their stories.
3. **Mood boards**: Create a visual board with inspiring images, colors, and textures that reflect your brand's aesthetic.

**Personal and Authentic Content**

1. **Meet the maker**: Share a bit about yourself, your inspiration, and your passion for jewelry making.
2. **Bloopers and outtakes**: Show the humorous side of your design process or studio mishaps.
3. **Personal stories**: Share how your jewelry has impacted your life or the lives of your customers.

**Other Ideas**

1. **Collaborations**: Partner with other handmade jewelry makers or influencers to create exclusive content.
2. **Tutorials**: Share simple jewelry-making techniques or styling tips.
3. **Giveaways**: Host a giveaway or contest to encourage engagement and attract new followers.

**Tips and Best Practices**

1. **Use relevant hashtags**: Add relevant hashtags to your Instagram Story to increase visibility.
2. **Keep it concise**: Keep your Story content brief and engaging, with a maximum of 15 seconds per clip.
3. **Use stickers and GIFs**: Add fun and interactive elements to your Story using stickers, GIFs, and emojis.
4. **Post consistently**: Maintain a consistent posting schedule to keep your followers engaged and interested in your content.
5. **Monitor and respond**: Keep an eye on your Story's performance and respond to comments and messages promptly.

By incorporating these ideas into your Instagram Story strategy, you'll be able to engage your followers, build a loyal community, and showcase your unique handmade jewelry brand.
user: I've been using Instagram stories to give my followers a sneak peek into my production process, and to ask for their feedback on new products. I've also been experimenting with polls, like the one I did last week where I asked my followers to vote for their favorite product design. Around 50 people voted, which was a great response.
assistant: That's fantastic! 🎉 You're doing a great job of engaging your followers and making them feel involved in your design process. By sharing behind-the-scenes content and asking for feedback, you're building a sense of community and fostering loyalty among your audience.

The poll you conducted is a great example of this. With 50 votes, you've not only generated engagement but also gathered valuable feedback that can inform your design decisions. This type of interaction can help you understand your audience's preferences and create products that resonate with them.

Here are a few suggestions to take your Instagram Stories to the next level:

1. **Use the "Question" sticker**: In addition to polls, you can use the "Question" sticker to ask open-ended questions and gather more detailed feedback from your followers.
2. **Share exclusive content**: Consider offering exclusive deals, promo codes, or early access to new products to your Instagram followers to make them feel special and valued.
3. **Create a series**: Develop a series of Instagram Stories that showcase a specific aspect of your production process, such as a "Design of the Week" or "Material of the Month" feature.
4. **Collaborate with followers**: Encourage followers to share their own jewelry-making experiences or tips, and re-share them on your Story. This can help create a sense of community and social proof.
5. **Utilize Instagram's features**: Experiment with Instagram's features like IGTV, Reels, and Live Streaming to mix up your content and keep your followers engaged.

Remember to track your Instagram Insights to see which types of content are performing well and adjust your strategy accordingly. Keep up the great work, and I'm sure you'll continue to see engagement and growth on your Instagram account! 💡
user: I'm planning to run another Facebook ad campaign soon, and I'd like to target people who have shown interest in similar products to mine. Can you give me some tips on how to effectively target my ad audience on Facebook?
assistant: Facebook ads can be a powerful way to reach potential customers who are interested in products like yours. Here are some tips to help you effectively target your ad audience on Facebook:

**1. Define Your Target Audience**

Before creating your ad campaign, identify your ideal customer based on demographics, interests, behaviors, and connections. Consider factors like:

* Age, gender, location, and language
* Interests, hobbies, and activities related to your product
* Behaviors, such as online shopping habits or purchase history
* Connections, like friends of fans or people who have engaged with your content

**2. Use Facebook's Audience Insights Tool**

Facebook's Audience Insights tool provides valuable information about your target audience, including demographics, interests, and behaviors. Use this tool to gain a deeper understanding of your audience and create more targeted ads.

**3. Leverage Lookalike Audiences**

Lookalike Audiences allow you to target people who are similar to your existing customers or followers. This can be a powerful way to reach new audiences who are likely to be interested in your product.

**4. Target Based on Interests**

Target people who have shown interest in products or topics related to yours. For example, if you sell handmade jewelry, you could target people who have liked pages related to fashion, accessories, or jewelry.

**5. Use Custom Audiences**

Custom Audiences allow you to target people who have engaged with your content, visited your website, or made a purchase from you in the past. This can help you retarget people who have already shown interest in your product.

**6. Exclude Unwanted Audiences**

Use Facebook's exclusion targeting feature to exclude people who are unlikely to be interested in your product. For example, if you're targeting people interested in handmade jewelry, you might exclude people who have shown interest in mass-produced jewelry.

**7. Monitor and Optimize**

Monitor your ad performance regularly and optimize your targeting based on the results. Identify which audiences are performing well and adjust your targeting accordingly.

**8. Use Facebook's Automatic Placements**

Facebook's Automatic Placements feature allows you to target multiple platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, and Audience Network, with a single ad campaign. This can help you reach a wider audience and increase ad visibility.

**9. Consider Retargeting**

Retargeting ads can be an effective way to reach people who have visited your website or engaged with your content but haven't converted yet. Use Facebook's retargeting feature to target these people and encourage them to take action.

**10. Test and Refine**

Test different targeting options and refine your approach based on the results. Continuously monitor your ad performance and adjust your targeting to optimize your ad spend and improve your ROI.

By following these tips, you can create targeted Facebook ad campaigns that reach people who are interested in products like yours and drive conversions for your business. Good luck with your ad campaign!
user: I'm planning to target people who have shown interest in similar products to mine, just like I did in my previous Facebook ad campaign, which reached around 2,000 people and resulted in 50 clicks to my website.
assistant: It sounds like you have a good understanding of your target audience and have had some success with Facebook ads in the past. Targeting people who have shown interest in similar products to yours is a great way to reach potential customers who are already interested in products like yours.

Considering your previous campaign reached 2,000 people and resulted in 50 clicks to your website, that's a decent click-through rate (CTR) of 2.5%. To improve upon this, you may want to consider a few things:

**1. Refine your targeting**: Take a closer look at the interests and behaviors of the people who clicked on your ad and converted. Are there any common themes or patterns? Use this information to refine your targeting and reach an even more specific audience.

**2. Optimize your ad creative**: Review your ad creative, including the image, headline, and copy. Is it eye-catching and compelling? Are you using social proof, such as customer testimonials or reviews, to build credibility? Consider testing different ad creative to see what resonates best with your audience.

**3. Improve your landing page experience**: When people click on your ad, they should be taken to a landing page that is relevant to the ad and provides a seamless user experience. Make sure your landing page is optimized for conversions, with a clear call-to-action (CTA) and minimal distractions.

**4. Consider increasing your budget**: If you're happy with the performance of your previous campaign, you may want to consider increasing your budget to reach a larger audience and drive more conversions.

**5. Monitor and optimize regularly**: Keep a close eye on your ad performance and make adjustments as needed. This could include pausing underperforming ads, adjusting your targeting, or testing new ad creative.

By refining your targeting, optimizing your ad creative, improving your landing page experience, and monitoring your performance regularly, you can continue to drive conversions and grow your business.

What do you think is the most important factor in driving conversions for your business? Is it the quality of your product, the strength of your brand, or something else entirely?

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

structured.fullText: {"notes":[{"candidate_claim_hints":["The user uses Instagram Stories to show production process sneak peeks and collect feedback on new products.","A recent Instagram Story poll received around 50 votes."],"confidence":0.96,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"subject"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I've been using Instagram stories to give my followers a sneak peek into my production process, and to ask for their feedback on new products.","evidence_reason":"The user explicitly describes how Instagram Stories are being used to engage followers and collect feedback.","importance":4,"keywords":["Instagram Stories","handmade jewelry","production process","feedback polls","50 votes","product design"],"note_type":"lesson","rationale":"This is reusable marketing context about a working content approach and observed engagement, not just a single atomic fact.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Instagram Stories strategy for the user's handmade jewelry marketing"},"summary":"For the handmade jewelry business, Instagram Stories are being used as an engagement and product-development channel by showing production-process sneak peeks, asking for feedback on new products, and running design polls;
